coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent detection of dinotefuran based on sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots and copper nanocluster hybrid
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yue
Wei, Qunyan
Zou, Tong
Kong, Yulin
Su, Linfeng
Ma, Dian
Wang, Yude
coredata.dc:description: Developing dinotefuran (DNF) sensing probe is crucial for the monitoring of DNF residues. A dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) and copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) hybrid (S-CQDs/CuNCs) were developed for sensitive and selective detection of DNF. The fluorescent signals of CuNCs were quenched in the presence of DNF, while the fluorescent signals of S-CQDs enhanced after the addition of DNF. The ratiometric fluorescent performance of as-developed probe was mainly based on the inner filter effect (IFE) between S-CQDs and CuNCs. Interestingly, the aggregation of CuNCs occurred after the addition of DNF, reducing the IFE effect between S-CQDs and CuNCs, leading to the fluorescent intensity of S-CQDs recovered and CuNCs fluorescence decayed. The ratiometric fluorescent performance of S-CQDs/CuNCs illustrated a good sensitivity and selectivity ranging from 10 μM and 500 μM, with a low detection limit of 7.04 μM. Importantly, real samples investigations implied the good application potential of S-CQDs/CuNCs in DNF monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Dinotefuran
S-CQDs
CuNCs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (inset: HRTEM) of S-CQDs (a) and CuNCs (c). The particle distribution (n = 40) (b) of S-CQDs (b) and CuNCs (d).
(a) XPS survey spectrum of S-CQDs. The high resolution spectra of C 1s (b), S 2p (c) and O 1s (d).

(a) UV-vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra of S-CQDs and CuNCs; (b) The emission spectra of S-CQDs at various excitation wavelengths; (c) The emission spectra of S-CQDs and S-CQDs/CuNCs with and without the addition of DNF; (d) Photoluminescence decay trace of S-CQDs in the presence and absence of CuNCs.
The fluorescence ratio of S-CQDs/CuNCs changed with the (a) CuNCs volume, (b) incubation time and (c) pH.
(a) Fluorescent response of S-CQDs/CuNCs probe mixed with various concentration of DNF (0–500 μM). (b) The ratiometric calibration curve of as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs ranging from 10 to 500 μM.
(a) Excitation curve of S-CQDs and the UV–vis spectra of S-CQDs/CuNCs probe and CuNCs in the presence and absence of DNF. (b) Zeta potential analyses of S-CQDs, CuNCs and S-CQDs/CuNCs.
TEM images of CuNCs in the absence (a) and presence of 400 μM of DNF.
Selectivity analyses of as-developed ratiometric fluorescent probe mixed various interfering chemicals (The concentration of pesticides and cations were set as 300 μM and 50 μM).
The schematic illustration of ratiometric determination of dinotefuran based on S-CQDs/CuNCs probe.
Comparison of various sensing platforms for the dinotefuran detection.
Recovery of dinotefuran determination in real samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent detection of dinotefuran based on sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots and copper nanocluster hybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Yunnan University, 650091, Kunming, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Yunnan University, 650091 Kunming, People’s Republic of China.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, Kunming, 650091, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, Kunming 650091, People’s Republic of China.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, 650091, Kunming, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials, Yunnan University, 650091, Kunming, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Center for International Research on Photoelectric and Energy Materials, Yunnan University, 650091 Kunming, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials and Energy, Yunnan University, 650091, Kunming, People’s Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: S-CQDs/CuNCs dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe is first time established for the quantitative sensing of DNF, and shows a good sensitivity and selectivity with a low detection limit of 7.04 μM, making it to be promising candidate for practical detectors for DNF.

Developing dinotefuran (DNF) sensing probe is crucial for the monitoring of DNF residues. A dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) and copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) hybrid (S-CQDs/CuNCs) were developed for sensitive and selective detection of DNF. The fluorescent signals of CuNCs were quenched in the presence of DNF, while the fluorescent signals of S-CQDs enhanced after the addition of DNF. The ratiometric fluorescent performance of as-developed probe was mainly based on the inner filter effect (IFE) between S-CQDs and CuNCs. Interestingly, the aggregation of CuNCs occurred after the addition of DNF, reducing the IFE effect between S-CQDs and CuNCs, leading to the fluorescent intensity of S-CQDs recovered and CuNCs fluorescence decayed. The ratiometric fluorescent performance of S-CQDs/CuNCs illustrated a good sensitivity and selectivity ranging from 10 μM and 500 μM, with a low detection limit of 7.04 μM. Importantly, real samples investigations implied the good application potential of S-CQDs/CuNCs in DNF monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Insecticides are indispensable to modern agriculture, which can prevent the damages of crops from pests. Unfortunately, it has been reported that the intensive use of insecticides have adverse effects to environment and ecosystem [1]. As a class of insecticides, neonicotinoids have gained a lot of attentions since it emerged in early 1990s [2]. Some studies have shown that the extensive usage of neonicotinoids are detrimental to pollinator insects [1,3]. Dinotefuran (DNF) is one of widely used neonicotinoid insecticides with broad-spectrum insecticidal activity. Previous report has indicated that the persistent and massive use of dinotefuran could lead to serious DNF insecticidal residues problem in fruits and vegetables, and the consumption of these foods could cause healthy issue to children and fetuses [4]. Besides, the risk assessment of DNF usage have indicated that the exposure of DNF adversely affect honeybees health [5]. It has been reported that honeybees are very important to agroecosystem, most of the flowering crops need to be pollinated by honeybees [6]. Therefore, it is necessary to monitor the residues of DNF for the safety concern. Considerable analytical strategies have been developed to determine dinotefuran, including gas chromatography, liquid chromatography and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [5]. However, these analytical techniques require expensive equipment and tedious sample pretreatments. Thus, the investigations on simple and cost-effective analytical methods present great value.

Fluorescent sensors have attracted a great deal of attentions in trace substance detection owing to their captivating advantages like high sensitivity, cost-efficient and simple pre-procedure [7]. These advantages enable fluorescent sensors to be promising candidate for the determination of heavy metal ions, pesticide residues and biomolecules [8–10]. After years of investigation and development, various fluorescent materials have been applied in fluorescent sensing field, such as semiconductor quantum dots [11], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [12], noble metal (gold, silver, copper) nanoclusters [13–15] and organic dyes [16]. Comparing with the fluorescent materials that mentioned above, carbon quantum dots were distinguished with excellent photostability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [17]. As a result of these standout properties, carbon quantum dots has been widely served as fluorescent sensor [12].

In most cases, the fluorescent detection process is performed by recording single or double (ratiometric) fluorescent irradiation signals. Although single irradiation fluorescent probe have been widely reported and employed in fluorescent trace subject detection, but the analytical results are easily affected by the sensing material’s concentration and incident light drift [18]. To evade such disadvantages, ratiometric fluorescent method was developed based on dual fluorescent emission signals [19]. The emergence of ratiometric fluorescent methods improved the contrast ratio of carbon quantum dots based probe in fluorescent assay. Owing to the self-calibration of carbon quantum dots based ratiometric fluorescent probe, the CQDs based ratiometric fluorescent probes were developed and applied to detect toxic metal ions, and biomolecule [20]. It has been reported that dual-emission CQDs and Au nanoclusters (AuNCs) hybrid ratiometric fluorescent probe were established for the determination of dopamine [18]. The analytical performances were evaluated by the fluorescent intensity changes of CQDs and AuNCs. The fluorescent signals of CQDs and AuNCs changed independently in two different patterns, the fluorescent intensity of CQDs enhanced after the addition of dopamine, while an obvious attenuation on AuNCs fluorescence could be observed after the addition of dopamine. The contrast of fluorescent signals changes between CQDs and AuNCs were conductive to the fluorescent discrimination in ratiometric detection process. Recently, Cu nanoclusters have drawn considerable attention in ratiometric fluorescent assay due to its low-cost and simple preparation. Therefore, the determination of DNF via ratiometric fluorescent method based on carbon quantum dots and Cu nanoclusters hybrid has great significance.

Scarcely any investigation was reported about the fluorescent detection of DNF. Inspired by this fact, in this study, S-CQDs/CuNCs fluorescent probe was established for the quantitative sensing of DNF. The ratiometric fluorescent assay for DNF is achieved by the aggregation of CuNCs reduced the IFE ability by CuNCs, resulting in the fluorescent signal of S-CQDs recover and the fluorescent signal of CuNCs decay. A wide dynamic range from 10 to 500 μM was obtained by the as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs probe. Furthermore, the as-developed ratiometric fluorescent probe presents good sensitivity and selectivity against various interfering substances. All the experimental analyses indicate that the as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs sensor holds great potential in DNF detection.

Citric acid, sodium hydroxide and sodium sulfide were obtained from Aladdin. Copper nitrate hydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O) and glutathione (GSH) were supplied by Adamas-beta. Dinotefuran was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology. All the insecticides for interfering investigation were obtained from Jiangsu Pesticide Research Institute used without further purification. The related interfering metal ion reagents were purchased from TianJin ZhiYuan Reagent Co. Ltd.

Blue fluorescence S-CQDs were synthesized facilely through hydrothermal process. Typically, 0.5 g of citric acid was dissolved in 50 mL of ultra-pure water under stirring. After that 0.1429 g of Na2S was mixed with the as-prepared citric acid solution under vigorous stirring. After 10 min stirring, the mixture were transferred into autoclave and heated at 190 °C for 12 h.

For the preparation of CuNCs, 250 mg of GSH was added in 50 mL of ultra-pure water, and then the solution were transferred into a refrigerator and refrigerated for 10 min. Then, 50 mg of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O was mixed with GSH solution under vigorous stirring in ice bath. After that, NaOH solution (1 M) was introduced to adjust the pH of the mixture and the mixture was stirred at 37 °C for 1 h, the corresponding pH value was 5.6. Finally, the resulting mixture solution was dialyzed (MW: 500 Da) against ultra-pure water for 3 h to remove unreacted precursors.

The morphology of as-prepared sensing probe was carried out by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM 2100). The UV–vis-1800 spectrophotometer (Jinghua Instrument, China) was used to determine the UV–vis absorption spectra of as-prepared sensing probe. Elemental and surface information of S-CQDs were conducted by the K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transformed infrared spectra (FT-IR, Avatar-360). The photoluminescence performance was recorded on Horiba Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer and the photoluminescence lifetime was conducted by Quantaurus-Tau fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Hamamatsu).

To a 5 mL volumetric test tube containing 50 μL of S-CQDs, 600 μL of CuNCs solution and 4.3 mL of 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.5) were added. After that, 50 μL of dinotefuran standard solution was injected into S-CQDs/CuNCs mixture and incubated for 7 min. The ratiometric analytical process of as-developed sensor was evaluated by measuring the fluorescent signal ratios (F1/F2) of S-CQDs (F1) and CuNCs (F2). The selective assay of the proposed platform was assessed by investigating the effects of various interfering ions and insecticides on the fluorescent ratios of S-CQDs/CuNCs probe.

To demonstrate the feasibility of as-developed ratiometric fluorescent probe, the analytical assay of DNF in commercially available honey were carried out. 1 g of honey was dissolved into 50 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the addition of certain concentration of DNF. In addition, the ratiometric determination of real samples were carried out by adding honey/DNF solution to as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs probe and the fluorescent spectra were recorded in the same conditions with Section 2.5.

TEM analyses were applied to investigate the morphologies and size of S-CQDs and CuNCs. The TEM images in Fig. 1 (a) and the particle distribution (Fig. 1(b)) indicated that the as-synthesized S-CQDs were well-dispersed in the range of 3.0–6.0 nm with the average diameter of 4.78 nm. In the HRTEM image of S-CQDs, the as-synthesized S-CQDs possess clear carbon lattice, which is close to (100) plane of graphitic carbon configuration [18,21]. As seen from the TEM and size distribution analyses of CuNCs (Fig. 1(c)–(d)), the results indicate that the as-synthesized CuNCs present a good dispersity and narrow particle distribution with an average particle diameter of 2.61 nm.

Thereafter, the elemental information and surfacely functional group of as-obtained S-CQDs were tested. As presented in Fig. 2 (a), the full XPS scan of S-CQDs exhibit three distinct binding energy states, which represent the contribution of S2p, C1 s and O1 s, respectively. For the deconvoluted spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2(b)), the binding energy state for S-CQDs revealed the contribution at 283.9, 284.6, 286.2 and 288.1 eV, corresponding to CSC, CC, CS and OCO, respectively [22–24]. The high resolution scan of S2p (Fig. 2(c)) with three different energy states at 163.6, 168.4 and 169.8 eV, which was related to the contribution of CSC 2p and CSO2 C (168.4, 169.8 eV), respectively [25]. As plotted in Fig. 2(d), the deconvoluted spectrum of O1 s was divided into three states at 530.9, 532.8 and 535.5 eV, which belong to the COOH, CO and CO bond, respectively.

FT-IR investigations of S-CQDs have been illustrated in Fig. 3 . According to the measurements, a typical hydroxyl (OH) vibration band at 3431 cm−1 can be observed [26]. The band around 2974 and 1644 cm−1 can be attributed to the CH and CC, respectively [25]. The absorption at 668 cm−1 suggested the presence of CS/CSC bending [26,27]. An obvious peak at 1053 cm−1 can be seen, which associated with the stretching of CS/SO [28]. Two peaks at 1570 and 1420 cm−1 were associated with the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of carboxylic groups (COO) [29]. The vibration of SH resulted a sharp peak around 2365 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum [30]. These results unveiled the S-doped nature in the carbon lattice configuration of S-CQDs.

In the present study, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was carried out by simply blending S-CQDs and CuNCs with different fluorescent signals. The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectrum of S-CQDs and CuNCs were recorded and plotted in Fig. 4 (a). From Fig. 4(a), an absorption peak around 329 nm can be seen, which related to n-π* transition of CO [31]. The absorption spectrum of CuNCs presents a strong absorption in UV region. Additionally, a blue emission of S-CQDs around 430 nm was observed, which was excited by 330 nm of light. As seen from Fig. 4(b), the fluorescent emissions of S-CQDs displayed an excitation-dependent property and this phenomenon was related to the surface defects of as-synthesized S-CQDs.

Previous reports have shown that CuNCs can be applied as fluorescent probe via FRET (Förster resonance energy transfer) and IFE [32,33]. Both FRET and IFE were referred to the adsorption band of quencher overlapped with the photoluminescence spectrum of fluorimetric substance, and the fluorescence intensity of fluorophore could be adjusted by the addition of quencher. FRET was related to the energy transfer between the quantum dots and quencher. The fluorescence lifetime of fluorescent substance could be adjusted by the inducing of quencher [34]. However, in a typical IFE process the excitation beam was filtered off or absorbed by quencher leaded to the attenuation of emission signal. In this specific situation, the quencher was served as an effective absorber and the excited radiation was absorbed by the excess quencher [34,35]. It was worthy to notice that IFE process was related to excitation re-absorption by second absorber. Therefore, the lifetime of fluorescent substance would not be changed. In the case of S-CQDs/CuNCs ratiometric fluorescence probe, the fluorescence signal of S-CQDs was quenched by the injection of CuNCs (Fig. 4(c)). For the better understanding of the possible quenching mechanism, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence lifetime decay analyses were carried out. As shown in Fig. 4(a), a small overlaps could be observed between the absorption spectrum of CuNCs and the photoluminescence curves of S-CQDs. While an obvious overlaps could be observed between the absorption band of CuNCs and the excitation spectrum of S-CQDs, which was conducted to the IFE process. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime decay was measured to investigate the interaction between S-CQDs and CuNCs (Fig. 4(d)). As illustrated in Fig. 4(d), the average fluorescent lifetime of S-CQDs in the absence and presence of CuNCs was calculated and a slightly decrease could be observed, which indicated that the possible fluorescence quenching of S-CQDs by CuNCs was mainly associated with the IFE process. The slight decrease of lifetime decay of S-CQDs was attributed to the weak FRET interaction between S-CQDs and CuNCs. The S-H moiety on the surface of S-CQDs has good affinity with Cu atoms, which was conducted to the interaction between S-CQDs and CuNCs. There was only a small apart of overlaps between the emission spectrum of S-CQDs and the absorption spectrum of CuNCs. This was one of the reasons for the weak FRET interaction between S-CQDs and CuNCs. Therefore, the fluorescent signal of S-CQDs was tuned by the IFE interaction between S-CQDs and CuNCs, the quenching mechanism was mainly attributed to the IFE process.

In this work, the fluorescent determination of DNF was conducted by the interaction between S-CQDs and CuNCs. Hence, experimental conditions like the content of CuNCs, detection pH and the incubation time were worthy of consideration. The analytical performance of S-CQDs/CuNCs was evaluated by the ratiometric fluorescence value F1/F2, where the F1 and F2 related to the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs and CuNCs. It could be seen in Fig. 5 (a), the ratiometric fluorescent value (F1/F2) decreased after the addition of 200 μL of CuNCs and remained stable in the presence of 600 μL CuNCs. Thereafter, the incubation time was investigated and shown in Fig. 5(b). A gradually increase of ratiometric fluorescence ratio can be observed with the extension of incubation time from 0 to 6 min. According to the time-dependent response of incubation time analyses, an equilibrium region of the incubation time and ratiometric fluorescence ratio was obtained after 7 min of incubation. Therefore, 7 min of incubation time was selected for the following tests. Additionally, the pH of sensing medium is crucial to the detection system. In the purpose of better analytical performance of as-developed probe for DNF detection, the influence of pH on sensing system was carried out and illustrated in Fig. 5(c). The analytical performance of as-developed probe in various pH buffer was evaluated by the fluorescent ratio changes of S-CQDs/CuNCs in the presence (red bar) and absence (yellow bar) of 200 μM DNF. As displayed in Fig. 5(c), the fluorescence ratio differences of the probe gradually increased when the pH varied from 3 to 6.5. However, when the pH exceeded 6.5, the fluorescence ratios differences of as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs were decayed. Hence, pH 6.5 was selected for the analytical detection pH for as-developed sensing probe. Furthermore, the pH-dependent performance of S-CQDs and CuNCs were evaluated and displayed as Fig. S1. The fluorescence signals of S-CQDs increased with the increase of pH from 2 to 6 and remained stable without significant changes thereafter pH 6. An obvious fluorescent decay could be observed in acidic medium, which could be assigned to the protonation process of surface carboxyl on S-CQDs, reduced the stability of as-synthesized S-CQDs. When the pH increased from 2 to 6, the deprotonation process of carboxyl increasingly became dominant. The saturated fluorescent intensity of S-CQDs in the pH range of 6–9 could be attributed to the deprotonation equilibrium of carboxyl present on the surface of S-CQDs [36]. Previous study has indicated that the fluorescence of metal nanoclusters was related to the donation of delocalized electrons of electron-rich atoms (e.g., N, O) or groups (e.g., COOH, NH2) of the ligands to the metal core [37]. Some reports have shown that GSH was bonded with CuNCs via Cu-S covalent bond in GSH stabilized CuNCs [38]. GSH contains sufficient carboxyl and amino groups in the molecular chain. Carboxyl groups could be protonated in acidic condition reduced the delocalization properties of carboxyl and resulted in weak donation of delocalized electrons to metal core. Thus, the fluorescence of CuNCs was decayed when the pH decreased from 5 to 2. Upon increasing the pH values from 5 to 9, a dramatic decrease in the fluorescent intensity of CuNCs was monitored as the pH varied. Besides, the fluorescence of CuNCs could be affected by the metallophilic attraction from Cu-Cu [39]. At higher pH values (5–9), carboxyl and amino groups was slowly deprotonated. The fluorescence intensity decayed with the pH increase. This could be a consequence of the strength of repulsive electrostatic force acting between neighboring GSH ligands, increased the Cu-Cu distance and resulted in the weaker Cu-Cu interaction and fluorescence at higher pH value [40]. As shown in Fig. 5(c), a larger fluorescence ratio difference in acidic condition could be observed. This phenomenon was related to the interaction between CuNCs and DNF. The as-synthesized CuNCs presented higher fluorescence intensity in acidic condition than alkaline condition. Furthermore, DNF could be protonated and present positive charge in acidic condition, which conducted to the interaction between negatively charged CuNCs and positively charged DNF, leading to larger fluorescence ratio difference. Previous report have shown that DNF was unstable in alkaline medium with hydrolysis process [41]. Therefore, the fluorescent ratio of S-CQDs/CuNCs was hardly changed in the presence of DNF in alkaline condition.

To the quantitative determine of DNF, the fluorescence spectrum and ratiometric fluorescent ratio of S-CQDs and CuNCs were obtained and depicted in Fig. 6 . The quantitative detection of DNF was evaluated by the fluorescent signal changes of S-CQDs and CuNCs. As illustrated in Fig. 6(a), the fluorescent signal of S-CQDs at 432 nm increased while that the fluorescent signal of CuNCs at 625 nm progressively decreased in the presence of various concentrations of DNF. The fluorescent signal of S-CQDs was quenched by IFE process between S-CQDs and CuNCs. The fluorescence turn on process of S-CQDs indicated that the presence of DNF reduced the IFE ability between S-CQDs and CuNCs, the corresponding color change image was presented in supplementary information as Fig. S2.

The ratiometric fluorescent detection for DNF was carried out by evaluating the fluorescent ratio value (F1/F2) of S-CQDS (F1) and CuNCs (F2). Fig. 6(b) presents the ratiometric fluorescence ratio of as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs probe change with the concentration of DNF. The fluorescent ratios were related to the DNF concentration, a good linear relationship (y = 0.7435 + 0.01136[DNF], R2 = 0.989) was acquired in the DNF concentration range from 10 to 500 μM, and the detection limit was calculated as 7.04 μM. These results indicate that the as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs sensor has highly sensitive and wide dynamic range on DNF. In addition, the maximum DNF allowable residual in vegetable permitted by China Food and Drug Administration is 9.89 μM [42]. Additionally, several DNF analytical methods were summarized in Table 1 for the comparison. As seen from Table 1, S-CQDs/CuNCs ratiometric fluorescent sensor presented a broad linear response range compared with other analytical methods listed in Table 1. Although the analytical methods like liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC–MS) and high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) could obtain low detection limits, but the corresponding dynamic response range was unsatisfied for the determination near the safe DNF residuals (9.89 μM). DNF is one of the low toxic insecticides that have been widely used in agricultural production. Given to the massive usage situation of DNF, the determination of DNF at high concentration also hold great significance. Therefore, the as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs ratometric fluorescent sensor was possessed with the potential in practical DNF determination.

To demonstrate the possible interaction between as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs ratiometric fluorescent sensor and DNF, UV–vis absorption spectrum and Zeta potential analyses of S-CQDs/CuNCs were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 7 (a), the UV–vis absorption curve of S-CQDs/CuNCs decayed after the addition of DNF, while background scattering at wavelength below 400 nm can be observed, which indicate that the aggregation process occurred after the addition of DNF. Further evidence for the interaction between DNF and as-developed ratiometric fluorescent probe is evaluated by Zeta potential analyses. As displayed in Fig. 7(b), the Zeta potential values of as-obtained carbon quantum dots and Cu nanoclusters present negative values, this result can be explained by the attachment of carboxyl groups on the surface [43]. After the introduction of CuNCs, an increment of Zeta potential value was observed, indicating the as-synthesized S-CQDs are stable with CuNCs. Namely, the photoluminescence decrease of S-CQDs by CuNCs was mainly attributed to the IFE between S-CQDs and CuNCs. However, after the addition of 100 and 400 μM DNF, the potential values of as-developed sensing system reduced from -15.8 to -6.1 mV, demonstrating that the stability of as-developed ratiometric fluorescent probe was decreased after the addition of DNF [44]. As depicted in Scheme 1 , it worthy to be notice that the fluorescence signals of S-CQDs were boosted with the injection of DNF, while the fluorescent signals of CuNCs were quenched in the presence of DNF. This phenomenon maybe attributed to the aggregation of CuNCs reduced the IFE ability between S-CQDs and CuNCs. Due to the aggregations of CuNCs, the photoluminescence intensity at 625 nm was tuned and resulted in the effective fluorescent quenching.

For the purpose of further investigate the aggregation process of CuNCs, the morphology of CuNCs without and with the addition of DNF was measured and exhibited in Fig. 8 . A significant aggregation phenomenon was presented in Fig. 8 in the presence of DNF. The aggregation of CuNCs in the presence of DNF can be attributed the special affinity between CuNCs and DNF. Zeta potential analysis has indicated that the surface of CuNCs and S-CQDs were negatively charged distribution. Hence, the Zeta potential value of as-established sensing probe was increased after the addition of CuNCs due to the electrostatic repulsion between S-CQDs and CuNCs. DNF was known as one of neonicotinoid insecticides which containing nitro and imino moieties, the corresponding chemical formula of DNF was presented in Scheme 1. The O atom of nitro moiety of DNF showed partial electron-negative charge [45]. It should be noted that electron-withdrawing nitro and imino moieties of DNF can be protonated in acidic medium, resulting the positively charged [41]. Moreover, the moieties on DNF have good affinity with Cu atoms which also contributed for the electrostatic interaction between CuNCs and DNF. Namely, DNF would preferentially contact with CuNCs when S-CQDs and CuNCs co-existed. Thus, an obvious electrostatic attraction could be observed between S-CQDs and DNF, resulting in the redistribution of the charges on the surface of CuNCs, which reduced the stability and presented aggregation of as-developed sensing probe. The donation of delocalized electrons of electron-rich atoms or groups of the ligands plays a major role to the fluorescence of metal nanoclusters [37]. Nitro and imino moieties in DNF is one of electron-withdrawing groups [41]. Those electron-withdrawing groups of DNF may absorb the delocalized electrons from the electron-rich groups on the surface of CuNCs to the metal core, which resulting in the weak fluorescence of CuNCs during the aggregation process. The fluorescence lifetime decay curve (Fig. S3) was recorded for the possible charge transfer illustration between CuNCs and DNF. It could be seen from Fig. S3 that the average lifetime of CuNCs was decayed in the presence of DNF. This result implied that some charge transferred from CuNCs to DNF after the addition of DNF. Furthermore, the overlaps between the excitation curve of S-CQDs and the absorption spectrum of CuNCs were decreased in the presence of DNF (Fig. 7(a)), which indicated that the aggregation of CuNCs reduced the absorption of excited radiation by CuNCs, resulted in weak IFE on S-CQDs. Therefore, the possible ratiometric fluorescent determination process for DNF can be illustrated as Scheme 1. The fluorescent signals of S-CQDs were quenched by the injection of CuNCs through IFE process. The analytical process of DNF determination was assigned to the aggregation of CuNCs by the electrostatic attraction between CuNCs and DNF. The fluorescence of CuNCs was quenched by the charge transfer between DNF and CuNCs during the aggregation process. This process reduced the IFE between S-CQDs and CuNCs, leading to S-CQDs fluorescence recovered and CuNCs fluorescent signal quenched.

Selectivity is one of vital analytical evaluation indicators for newly developed ratiometric fluorescent probe. Therefore, the selectivity of as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs system was investigated and plotted in Fig. 9 . Several potential interfering substances including Zn2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Na+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, fluazinam, dursban, acetamiprid, imidacloprid, carbendazim, thiamethoxam were investigated for the DNF determination. As seen from Fig. 9, the ratio of F1/F2 was unchanged after the addition of possible interfering chemicals, and a significant increase was obtained in the presence of (300 μM) DNF. Thus, the abovementioned results indicated that the as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs probe displayed a good selectivity for the DNF determination. The excellent selectivity of S-CQDs/CuNCs probe on DNF might be attributed to the good affinity between CuNCs and the nitrogen atoms in DNF. Therefore, the as-developed ratiometric fluorescent probe illustrates an excellent sensitivity and selectivity on DNF.

Previous reports have shown that DNF is toxic to honeybees and the use of DNF may pose a risk of excessive DNF residues in honey. In order to investigate the applications of as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs probe in real samples, the commercially available honey was selected for the determination of DNF. Certain amount of DNF (50 μM, 100 μM and 200 μM, respectively) were injected into honey solution, the recoveries of spiked samples were calculated and shown in Table 2 . The recovery ratios of honey samples were 93 % to 112 %. Previous study has shown that honey was not a vitamin-rich food, the main ingredients of honey were saccharides (glucose, fructose), water and trace amounts of group B vitamins [46]. According to previous reports, only few milligrams of vitamins can be found in 100 g of honey [47,48]. In order to further investigate the possible interference in real samples analyses, glucose, fructose, vitamin C, vitamin B2 and vitamin B3 were took consideration. As shown in Fig. S4 of supportive information, among of these possible interferences only slight influence on the fluorescence ratios could be observed. Overall, real sample analyses of DNF in honey was indicated that the as-developed ratiometric fluorescent S-CQDs/CuNCs probe had certain feasibility for the determination of DNF in actual conditions.

In summary, we have developed a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent platform based on fluorescent S-CQDs and CuNCs for the determination of DNF. The fluorescent signals of S-CQDs were reduced by CuNCs via IFE and recovered in the presence of DNF. After the addition of DNF, CuNCs aggregated and reduced the fluorescent intensity of CuNCs. The aggregation of CuNCs decreased the IFE ability between S-CQDs and CuNCs, resulting the S-CQDs fluorescence recovery and CuNCs fluorescence decay. The as-developed ratiometric fluorescent probe presented an excellent selectivity and analytical performance in the DNF range from 10 to 500 μM with a low detection limit as 7.04 μM. In addition, real sample measurements indicated that the as-developed S-CQDs/CuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe present potential applications in actual DNF determinations. All the experimental analyses demonstrate that as-developed fluorescent probe is a promising candidate for ratiometric DNF detection.

Yue Yang: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Qunyan Wei: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Tong Zou: Data curation, Methodology. Yulin Kong: Data curation, Methodology. Linfeng Su: Data curation, Methodology. Dian Ma: Data curation, Methodology. Yude Wang: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61761047 and No. 41876055), Department of Science and Technology of Yunnan Province via the Key Project for the Science and Technology (Grant No. 2017FA025) and Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Yunnan Province. This work also Supported by Yunnan University’s Research Innovation Fund for Graduate Students (Grant No. 2019z036).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128534.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yue Yang received his M.E. degree in Department of Materials Science and Engineering from Yunnan University in 2017. Currently, he is currently a graduate student in Yunnan University and devotes to carbon quantum dots and their applications in fluorescent sensors.

Qunyan Wei received her B. Eng. degree in electrochemistry from Harbin Institute of Technology In 1986,her M.S. degree in precious metallurgy from Kunming Institute of Precious Metals in 1989, and Ph.D. degree in metallurgy engineering from Kunming University of Science and Technology in 2007. She is currently an associate professor at Yunnan University. Her research interests include the design and synthesis of novel nanostructured materials and their applications.
Tong Zou is currently a graduate student in Yunnan University and devotes to quantum dots and their applications in fluorescent sensors.

Yulin Kong received his B.S. degree in School of Materials Science and Engineering from North University of China in 2019. He is currently a graduate student in Yunnan University and devotes to inorganic functional materials and their applications in sensors.

Linfeng Su received his B.S. degree in School of Materials Science and Engineering from Anhui University in 2018. He is currently a graduate student in Yunnan University and devotes to inorganic functional materials and their applications in sensors.

Dian Ma received his B.S. degree in School of Materials Science and Engineering from China Three Gorges University in 2019. He is currently a graduate student in Yunnan University and devotes to inorganic functional materials and their applications in sensors.

Yude Wang obtained his M.S. degree in Physics Condensed State from Yunnan University in 1997 and Ph.D. in Materials Physics and Chemistry from Tsinghua University in 2003. From 2005 to 2007, he was a guest scientist in Max-Planck-Institute of Metal and an Alexander von Humboldt fellow in Max-Planck-Institute of Colloids and Interfaces, Germany. Currently, he is a professor at the Department of Physics, Yunnan University. His work is devoted to chemical and biochemical sensors, nanostructured functional materials and their applications.
